channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Israel Commits to New Lebanon Cease-Fire, Ambassador Says, but Troops Will Remain
item.description: The fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon had delayed the next stage of talks to end the U.S.-Iranian war. Hezbollah did not immediately comment.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Game Changer’? Too Soon to Tell. But Ukraine Flexed in Striking Moscow.
item.description: The drone attack that sent plumes of smoke rising over Moscow intensified Ukrainian hopes of bringing the war to Russia.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady
item.media:description: Smoke rising from an oil refinery in Moscow after a Ukrainian drone attack on Thursday, in an image obtained from social media.

item.title: Mexico’s Laws Have a New Target: Journalists
item.description: Politicians and officials in Mexico are using the country’s laws to intimidate critics and the media, forcing them into censorship and blunting scrutiny.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega and Paulina Villegas
item.media:credit: Marian Carrasquero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Élfego Riveros, a reporter in Teocelo, Mexico, whose satirical spot for a community-run radio station resulted in five reporters being found guilty of gender-based political violence.

item.title: U.S. to End AIDS Funding for South Africa
item.description: According to an unsigned statement from the State Department, the U.S. will phase out support for H.I.V. prevention and treatment in South Africa.
item.dc:creator: Zimasa Matiwane
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:description: A men’s health clinic in Johannesburg, South Africa, that closed last year after President Trump’s executive order slashed foreign aid.

item.title: Burnham’s Stunning Victory in Makerfield Election May Trigger Starmer’s Last Stand
item.description: Andy Burnham, a Labour mayor, won a resounding majority in a special election on Thursday, making him eligible to challenge Prime Minister Keir Starmer for the country’s leadership.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Andy Burnham speaking to his supporters on Friday after winning a seat in Parliament with almost 55 percent of the vote.

item.title: Italy Doesn’t ‘Beg,’ Giorgia Meloni Tells Trump
item.description: President Trump said the Italian leader had “begged” him to take a photo together at the Group of 7 summit in France. The remarks come as their once-friendly relationship has frayed.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni of Italy after a Group of 7 photo in Evian-les-Bains, France, on Tuesday.

item.title: Moscow Refinery Blast May Have Been Caused by Friendly Fire, Video Suggests
item.description: Footage shows a Russian air defense missile traveling toward a fuel silo, which erupts in an explosion around the time of its arrival.
item.dc:creator: James McManagan, Paul Sonne and Malachy Browne

item.title: Catherine, Princess of Wales Questions Digital Screens’ Role in Children’s Lives
item.description: The princess published an essay Friday warning against a childhood “mediated by screens,” days after Britain announced a ban on social media for children under 16.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: Catherine, Princess of Wales, visiting a municipal preschool during the first day of her visit to Reggio Emilia in May in Reggio Emilia, Italy.

item.title: Canadian Billionaire Frank Stronach Is Convicted of Sexual and Indecent Assault
item.description: Mr. Stronach, 93, was found guilty of sexual assault and indecent assault in two cases and acquitted on three other charges.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Chris Young/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Frank Stronach arriving at a Toronto court on Friday.

item.title: Trump’s Iran Deal Is Off to a Chaotic Start
item.description: The preliminary agreement, not yet a week old, has sown confusion: nixed Swiss signing ceremonies, rifts with Israel and Congress, and uncertainty over new talks.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Part of the Bürgenstock resort in Switzerland, where Swiss officials had hoped to host new peace negotiations between the U.S. and Iran.

item.title: A Missing Ingredient in Ukraine’s Barrages on Russia: Ballistic Missiles
item.description: Ukrainian drone attacks have achieved breakthroughs, but only ballistic missiles can push Russia to reconsider its war objectives, military experts and officials say.
item.dc:creator: Constant Méheut
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Moscow after a Ukrainian drone attack on Thursday, in an image from social media.

item.title: Extreme Heat Warnings Issued Across Europe: What to Know
item.description: Unusually high temperatures are expected across parts of Western Europe into early next week, weather agencies said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel

item.title: How Quickly Could Andy Burnham Become U.K. Prime Minister?
item.description: While Mr. Burnham’s leadership ambitions are clear, Prime Minister Keir Starmer has stated that he would fight any challenge.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham speaking after his victory in a special parliamentary election, on Friday in Ashton-in-Makerfield, England.

item.title: Back After Vanishing for 2 Weeks, Russian Banker Tries to Project Calm
item.description: The central bank leader Elvira S. Nabiullina reappeared for a news conference after a gap that highlighted deep tensions in the country’s political and economic elite.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Russian Central Bank Press Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Elvira S. Nabiullina, the governor of Russia’s central bank, spoke on Friday. The bank’s press office issued this photograph of the occasion.

item.title: Cape Verde Will Count on Vozinha Against Uruguay at the World Cup
item.description: Cape Verde’s veteran goalkeeper, Vozinha, is keeping the archipelago nation’s hopes outsized after his performance against soccer giant Spain.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean, Andre Amaral and Saikou Jammeh
item.media:description: Cape Verde’s veteran goalkeeper, Josimar José Évora Dias, after the team played to a scoreless tie with Spain on Monday.

item.title: A 175-Year-Old Shipwreck, Lots of Booze and a Korean Island’s Quest to Survive
item.description: A South Korean island is reviving a long‑forgotten episode involving a French whaler as a wine festival, in an effort to stay relevant.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun and Woohae Cho

item.title: Charles Kushner, U.S. Ambassador to France, Riles Paris With Trump Diplomacy
item.description: The U.S. envoy to Paris has emerged as the embodiment of combative diplomacy in the age of President Trump, riling the French establishment notably with his accusations of antisemitism.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: Charles Kushner, the U.S. ambassador to France, greeting President Trump on Monday at the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France.

item.title: Lebanon Emerges as Weak Link in U.S.-Iran Deal to End War
item.description: The conflict between Israel and Hezbollah, once seen as a secondary front to the American-Israeli war on Iran, has become one of the main obstacles to ending it.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Christina Goldbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Outside the grave of the former Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah, who was killed in an Israeli strike, in Beirut on Tuesday.

item.title: Trains Collide in UK, Killing at Least One and Injuring Dozens of Others
item.description: At least one person was killed and dozens of others were injured in the crash near Bedford, England, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:description: Emergency service workers at the scene of a collision involving two trains near Bedford, England, on Friday.

item.title: Lebanon Cease-Fire Bolsters U.S.-Iran Truce, After a Shaky Start
item.description: Talks on a long-term peace deal were set to begin on Friday, but Iran pulled out, citing Israeli strikes in Lebanon. Hours later, diplomats say, Israel and Hezbollah agreed to halt fighting there.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Euan Ward, Adam Rasgon, Johnatan Reiss and Richard Pérez-Peña
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Cleaning up after Israeli strikes in Touline, southern Lebanon, on Tuesday.

item.title: Woman Receives 12-Year Sentence for 1978 Scalding Death of Stepdaughter, 5
item.description: Janice Nix was found guilty of manslaughter after her stepson told the police that she had forced his sister into a hot bath as a form of punishment in their home in South London.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins

item.title: How Cubans Are Living in the Dark
item.description: Lisette Poole González, a photographer for The New York Times, talks with Jack Nicas, The Times’s Mexico City bureau chief, about photographing Havana at night amid an energy crisis.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas, Lisette Poole González, Leila Medina, Christina Thornell, Coleman Lowndes, Joey Sendaydiego and Thomas Vollkommer

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Says He Dislikes Deal With U.S., but Allowed It
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei distanced himself from the agreement, putting responsibility for it on Iran’s president, and said it does not mean acceding to U.S. demands.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Photos of Mojtaba Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader, are held up during a memorial for victims of the war, in Tehran in May.

item.title: Our Allies Are Wondering Whether Supporting the American War Machine Is Worth It
item.description: Is the Iran war an early sign of “superpower suicide"?
item.dc:creator: Linda Kinstler
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Deadly Violence Rocks Lebanon in Threat to U.S.-Iran Deal
item.description: Israel launched strikes across the country after Iran-backed Hezbollah said it had ambushed Israeli troops, leaving diplomats scrambling to prevent the deal from collapsing.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke following Israeli bombardment in southern Lebanon on Friday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Adam Rasgon, Johnatan Reiss and Euan Ward

item.title: Swiss Officials Say Iran Talks Are Off, for Now
item.description: The announcement raised immediate questions over the fate of the agreement President Trump signed this week, with key details left to be worked out.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pedestrians in Tehran on Monday. Talks on issues including Iran’s nuclear program have been postponed.

item.title: What Does It All Mean? Once a Year, French Students Try to Explain.
item.description: The high school philosophy exam is a rite of passage for French students. This year included questions about Friedrich Nietzsche’s 1878 book, “Human, All Too Human.”
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Juliette Pavy for The New York Times
item.media:description: High school students in Paris preparing for a philosophy exam, in June.

item.title: Who Is Andy Burnham, the Man Who Could Be Britain’s Next Prime Minister?
item.description: Charismatic, northern and exuding a relaxed optimism, Mr. Burnham is a contrast to Keir Starmer. His allies hope he could mend Labour’s relationship with voters.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, Labour’s most popular politician, arriving on Friday for the results announcement for the Makerfield by-election, in which he beat the populist right-wing Reform U.K. party.

item.title: Burnham Wins Makerfield By-Election, Clearing Path to Challenge Starmer as UK Prime Minister
item.description: Andy Burnham,
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear

item.title: Vance’s Defense of Iran Deal Rests on Vague and Misleading Claims
item.description: The vice president said the United States had leverage to dictate the outcome of the next round of negotiations. But he claimed incorrectly that Iran got no new benefit from the lifting of oil sanctions.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Rebecca F. Elliott and Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance during a briefing at the White House on Thursday.

item.title: Man Forced Boy, 3, Into Crocodile Pen at English Zoo, Police Say
item.description: A man is being held on suspicion of attempted murder after a boy was critically injured when he ended up in a crocodile enclosure at a zoo outside Cambridge, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:description: The Johnsons of Old Hurst says it is home to more than 100 animals, including crocodiles, African lions and Bengal tigers.

item.title: Fallout From the Iran Deal
item.description: Trump’s agreement reads like a list of concessions to Iran. Will it damage him politically?
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Vance Lashes Out at Israeli Critics of U.S.-Iran Agreement to End the War
item.description: The vice president said that the United States was the only powerful ally Israel had left and noted that two-thirds of the weapons that protected Israel were paid for by U.S. tax dollars.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan, Erica L. Green, Max Bearak and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance during a news briefing at the White House on Thursday. He lashed out at critics of President Trump’s deal with Iran.

item.title: Gunmen Attack a Major Airport in Niger
item.description: A militant group claimed responsibility for the early-morning assault on Niamey airport, the second such attack in the West African country this year.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.media:description: The airport in Niamey, Niger, in 2023.

item.title: 19-Year-Old Suspect in Consulate Shooting Arrested at Toronto Airport
item.description: The teen had fled from the police during a raid at an apartment building that set off a shootout, which left an officer dead and another suspect injured.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Toronto Pearson Airport last year. A man wanted for firing shots at the U.S. Consulate in Toronto was arrested here on Thursday.

item.title: The Major Oak, Ancient Tree of Robin Hood Legend, Has Died
item.description: The Major Oak in the Sherwood Forest was between 800 and 1,200 years old. It succumbed to a combination of over-tourism, climate change and misguided efforts to save it.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Royal Society for the Protection of Birds
item.media:description: The Major Oak in Sherwood Forest Nature Reserve in Nottinghamshire, England, in May.

item.title: Iran and the U.S. Have an Understanding. Will It Lead to a Deal?
item.description: Europe and the larger world will be watching carefully to see if talks produce a lasting agreement on Iran’s nuclear program and the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: President Trump in Évian-les-Bains, France, for the Group of 7 meeting on Wednesday.

item.title: Many World Cup Fans Would Rather Skip the Hydration Breaks
item.description: It’s hot in North America in June, but fans are griping that the breaks that have been added to each half are an excuse for the networks to show more ads.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: Even in Toronto, World Cup play is paused once each half for a “hydration break.”

item.title: In Ancient Pits Near Stonehenge, Scientists See Hints of Solstice Ritual
item.description: British archaeologists may have found the remains of a site where people celebrated the solstice thousands of years ago, a few miles from the famed stone circle.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg

item.title: London Hospital Worker Tried to Sell Princess Catherine’s Medical Records, Reports Say
item.description: Britain’s data regulator said it had reprimanded a health worker for misusing sensitive information. The hospital said the person no longer worked there.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Chris Jackson/Chris Jackson Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Catherine, Princess of Wales in Windsor, England, on Monday.

item.title: Gulf States Are Frustrated by Failure to Tackle Iran’s Missiles, Analysts Say
item.description: The preliminary U.S.-Iranian peace deal does not address Iranian rockets or drones, raising questions in the region about relying on Washington as a security guarantor.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard with a photo of a missile and a message saying “Every missile has one message” in Tehran earlier this year.

item.title: Afghanistan Issues Ban on Smartphones for Civil Servants and Military
item.description: Those using their phones at work will see them smashed and face punishment, according to a written order from the Taliban government’s highest court.
item.dc:creator: Safiullah Padshah, Yaqoob Akbary, Attaullah Omar and Elian Peltier
item.media:description: A Taliban security officer using a mobile phone in Kandahar, Afghanistan, last year. Civil servants and members of the country’s armed forces have been told not to bring smartphones to work.

item.title: Hegseth Berates NATO Allies for ‘Shameful’ Response to U.S. War in Iran
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth used a meeting of NATO defense ministers to continue the Trump administration’s sharp criticism of European military policies.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth during a NATO meeting on Thursday.

item.title: Israel Stunned by Trump’s Iran Deal
item.description: The agreement accomplishes none of Israel’s stated war aims and arguably leaves the country in worse shape on each of them.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel in Jerusalem on Monday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green, Max Bearak, Jonathan Swan and Rebecca F. Elliott

item.title: Archbishop of Canterbury Apologizes for Church of England’s Role in Forced Adoptions
item.description: Tens of thousands of babies were taken from unmarried women and girls from the 1950s to the 1980s. The Church of England apologized for the “pain, shame and indignity” the practice inflicted.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: The archbishop of Canterbury, Sarah Mullally, in April. In her apology, she said that the forced adoptions had taken place “in a society that often valued secrecy and respectability over compassion and care.”

item.title: As India Temporarily Bans Telegram, Which Other Countries Have Restricted the Messaging App?
item.description: Several countries have restricted the messaging app, a valuable platform for dissidents, criminals and extremists. Here’s an overview.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times

item.title: Iran Gets Major Economic Lifeline for Minimal Concessions in Initial Deal
item.description: The agreement delays the most difficult steps for Iran for later talks, while granting it crucial benefits.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A satellite image of Kharg Island, a crucial Iranian oil hub. The deal grants Iran waivers to begin exporting its oil even before the negotiation of a final agreement on its nuclear program.

item.title: Ukraine Strikes Moscow Refinery in Large-Scale Drone Attack
item.description: The attack, which shut down the capital’s airports for several hours, was part of an escalating campaign to bring the conflict home to Russians.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: The scene over the area of an oil refinery on the outskirts of Moscow on Thursday.

item.title: What to Know About Canada’s New Citizenship Eligibility Requirements and How to Apply
item.description: Six months after Canada expanded its citizenship eligibility, requests are flooding in for archival records. The documents are vital for proving ties to the country.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bound historical parish registers at the Anglican Diocese in Toronto, Ontario.

item.title: Critics of Russia Say This Critic Isn’t Critical Enough
item.description: Alexander Sokurov has questioned the Russian president about government repression, but he is still controversial among his country’s exiles.
item.dc:creator: Milana Mazaeva and Neil MacFarquhar
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alexander Sokurov during the screening of his latest film, “Director’s Diary,” in Paris, in March.

item.title: Taiwan’s President Says He Trusts Trump to Approve Arms Sales
item.description: President Lai Ching-te made the hopeful remarks after Mr. Trump suggested that he could use a proposed $14 billion arms package for Taiwan as leverage with Beijing.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:description: President Lai Ching-te of Taiwan at a news conference for foreign journalists in Taipei on Thursday.

item.title: In Southeast Asia, Switching to Solar Offers Relief from Energy Crisis Caused by Iran War
item.description: Homeowners and businesses in Southeast Asia, which relies heavily on imported oil, are installing rooftop solar panels to reduce the sting of soaring energy costs.
item.dc:creator: Zunaira Saieed and Aie Balagtas See
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Setting up a solar power system at a home in Quezon City, Philippines, this month.

item.title: Why the By-Election in Makerfield Will Have Giant Consequences for the UK
item.description: Here is what to know about the by-election on Thursday in Makerfield, the outcome of which could lead to Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s ouster.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, the mayor of Greater Manchester, giving a speech in Wigan, England, on Saturday.
